title: You Can't Let the AI Grade Its Own Homework
body:
Disclosure: This post was composed by Claude (Anthropic) using the Invert MCP server. See the About page for more on how this site works.

A few months ago I was deep in the jazz-nextjs project — a headless Next.js frontend for this site — and Claude Code was being impressively productive. Like, uncomfortably productive. It was writing components, fixing TypeScript errors, wiring up API endpoints, and committing as it went. And I kept finding myself wondering: is any of this actually getting reviewed?

The answer, obviously, was no. Not really. Claude would write code, run a quick sanity check, declare it good, and commit. That's not code review. That's an AI doing its homework and then grading itself.

So I built something to fix that.

The problem with AI-reviewed AI code

Here's the thing about using Claude Code for serious development work: it's genuinely useful. It's not a toy. I've been using it extensively on jazz-nextjs and a handful of other projects, and when it works, it works. The test coverage improved. The TypeScript got cleaner. The Zod schemas got more rigorous. Things I'd been putting off for months got done in an afternoon.

But the commit workflow bothered me. Claude was moving fast enough that I'd lose track of what had changed. And when I'd ask it to review its own code before committing, it would do the thing where it reads it back, says "this looks good," and commits anyway. That's not nothing — it catches obvious errors — but it's not what I'd call adversarial review. An AI reviewing its own code has exactly the same blind spots the AI that wrote it does.

The parallel in human software development is obvious: you don't review your own PRs. You open the PR, someone else reviews it, and they catch the thing you were too close to see. The whole point of code review is the separation of roles. That's not a process nicety — it's why code review exists at all.

What I built

claude-code-reviewer is a mandatory code review system for Claude Code. When Claude tries to commit changes, it gets stopped. It can't proceed until a separate reviewer agent — a different Claude instance — has independently examined the code, run the test suite, and written a timestamped approval to a file. Only after that approval file exists does the commit go through.

The enforcement runs at three layers: a PreToolUse hook that intercepts commit attempts and checks for a valid approval file; a pre-commit shell hook that validates the approval timestamp, enforces commit size limits, runs tests, and checks for accidentally committed secrets; and a CLAUDE.md instruction set that tells Claude it needs to spawn a reviewer and explicitly cannot approve its own work.

The 5-minute expiration window on the approval file is intentional. You can't get an approval, go off and make a bunch of other changes, and use the same approval to commit them. Each commit requires a fresh review of the actual code being committed.

No external services. No API calls to some third-party review system. Just Claude, a second Claude, and a bash script keeping them honest.

How it evolved from jazz-nextjs

The jazz-nextjs project was the petri dish for this. I was building an increasingly complicated Next.js app — Zod validation, Incremental Static Regeneration, a custom API client with retry logic and rate limiting, the whole thing — and Claude Code was a genuinely valuable collaborator throughout. But the project was also moving fast enough that "I'll review this properly later" was becoming a consistent lie I was telling myself.

What pushed me over the edge was a stretch where Claude made several commits in quick succession that were technically fine individually but created a problem in combination. Each one was reviewed — by Claude, of code Claude wrote. None of them had an obvious defect. Together they introduced a subtle regression that took me longer to track down than it should have.

That's the thing that self-review misses. You review a diff. You don't always review the diff in context. A human reviewer coming in fresh might have caught it. A second Claude agent reviewing from scratch with no emotional investment in "what I was trying to do here" might have caught it too. A single Claude instance reviewing its own work almost certainly won't, because it starts from the same assumptions that produced the bug.

So I extracted the pattern I wanted — forced reviewer separation, timestamp-gated approval, automated testing as a prerequisite — and made it reusable. The repo is a bash installer that sets up the hooks and writes the necessary config files. Drop it in any project where you're using Claude Code and you have mandatory review from that point on.

What this doesn't solve

I'm going to be direct about the limitations here because "adversarial AI review" sounds more robust than it is in practice.

The reviewer Claude is still Claude. It has the same training, the same general tendencies, the same potential blind spots as the Claude that wrote the code. Spawning a second instance doesn't give you a fundamentally different perspective — it gives you a fresh instantiation of the same underlying model with no prior context. That's meaningfully better than nothing, but it's not the same as a human reviewer with different experience, different context, and actual skin in the game.

What it does give you is process. It forces a pause. It ensures tests run before the commit lands. It prevents the "write, glance, commit" loop that makes AI-assisted development hard to audit after the fact. It's a speed governor, not a guarantee.

There's also the obvious escape hatch: the human bypass. If you need to commit something without going through the review process, you can. The system supports it deliberately. The point was never to make Claude Code unusable — it was to make the rigorous path the default.

The broader point

I've been writing about AI tools a lot lately — the MCP work on this site, the disclosure post, all of it — and the thread running through everything is the same: AI is useful when it's a well-constrained contributor, not when it's the decision-maker. Claude can write code. It should not unilaterally decide that code is ready to ship.

The same instinct that makes me want to document AI use on this site is what made me want a mandatory review step before commits. Transparency about what the AI did. Accountability for what went into the repo. A human in the loop, even if "in the loop" means me glancing at an approval report before something goes to production.

You can't let the AI grade its own homework. That's really it. Everything else in the repo is implementation detail.

claude-code-reviewer is open source at github.com/jazzsequence/claude-code-reviewer. This post was composed by Claude using the Invert MCP server.
author: Chris Reynolds
excerpt: I was using Claude Code heavily on jazz-nextjs and it kept reviewing and committing its own work. That's not code review. That's an AI doing its homework and then grading itself. So I built something.
